meta.title: CIRO Supervisor Exam Syllabus — structured content model
meta.note: Outcome statements condensed from the official syllabus (verbatim-faithful scope; full parameter text in research/02-syllabus-study-guide-competency-profile.md; verbatim English text in research/sources/Supervisor-Exam-Syllabus-EN.txt). All 103 outcome IDs and taxonomy levels verified against the official English PDF on 2026-06-04. Taxonomy levels are cumulative.

elements.title: General regulatory framework
elements.summary: Regulators in Canadian investment services and consumer protections through legislation (Criminal Code, PCMLTFA); applying complaint-handling procedures; analyzing conduct that could breach CIRO standards of conduct and conflicts of interest rules.

outcomes.statement: Role and authority of the CSA and provincial/territorial securities and derivatives regulators: jurisdiction, mandate, forms of legislation (NIs, MIs, National Policies, Staff Notices, Companion Policies), registration requirements, enforcement powers.

Role and authority of CIRO: jurisdiction, recognition orders and delegated authority, mandate, dealer registration and individual approval, IDPC Rules, UMIR, role of rules/guidance/forms (standards of conduct, sales practices, external communications), enforcement powers.
Registration requirements for Investment Dealers and approval of individuals; roles of CSA and CIRO in that process.
Role and authority of exchanges and other marketplaces: exchanges, ATSs, crypto-asset trading platforms, Foreign Organized Regulated Markets.
Role and authority of clearing houses: CDS and CDCC.
Role of CIPF: purpose, dealer funding requirements, governance, role in dealer bankruptcy/insolvency including pooling of client assets.
Function of other financial regulators/agencies: FSRA, Bank of Canada, RCMP IMET, FINTRAC, OSFI, privacy commissioners, OBSI, and US/foreign regulators (SEC, FINRA, CFTC, NFA).
Purpose and implications of federal statutes: Bank Act (stability, consumer protection, competition); Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act Part XII (bankruptcy of a securities firm).

Criminal Code application to financial crimes: fraud, theft, theft by power of attorney, misappropriation, false statements/pretenses, obtaining execution of a security by fraud, forgery, uttering forged documents, false prospectus, organized crime offences, insider trading.

PCMLTFA and Regulations: stages of money laundering, compliance program requirements, AML policies and procedures, client due diligence, enterprise risk assessment and risk indicators, employee training, business relationship record-keeping.
Other applicable laws: CASL, confidentiality/non-disclosure agreements, PIPEDA, public company disclosure rules and statutory shareholder rights.
The Investment Dealer's obligations to clients and others: legislative, contractual, organizational factors.

Client complaint requirements in specific situations: reporting requirements and failure to report, internal investigation obligation, settlement agreements, complaint policies and procedures, roles of CIRO/OBSI/provincial regulators, designated complaints officer, alternative resolution processes.
Recourse available to dissatisfied clients: arbitration, litigation, OBSI, CIRO.
Situations requiring CIRO's whistleblower service for non-client matters: internal misconduct/illegal activity, potential systemic wrongdoing, potential securities frauds.
Consequences/liabilities of settlement agreements entered into with clients without dealer approval; penalties regulators may apply.

Role of ethics and personal integrity of Supervisors: link between ethical behaviour and compliance (due care, independent judgment, trustworthiness, honesty, professionalism); CIRO standards of conduct including conduct unbecoming and just and equitable principles of trade.
Ethics and integrity in corporate governance: transparency, resolution of compensation conflicts, oversight and accountability, training and awareness.
Consequences and risks of unethical behaviour: legal, reputational, regulatory scrutiny, employee morale/turnover, client trust and retention.

CIRO conflicts-of-interest requirements: avoid/disclose/manage/resolve material conflicts; standards for producing Supervisors; identifying compensatory, managerial, ownership conflicts; protection of corporate assets; confidentiality; fair dealing.
Outside activities and positions of influence: prohibition/approval/disclosure/required action; existing and reasonably foreseeable material conflicts; written disclosure; reporting obligations.
Prohibitions and exemptions on personal financial dealings with clients: acceptance of consideration, settlement agreements, borrowing from clients, lending to clients, control or authority.

Containing confidential or material non-public information: who is likely to receive it (investment banking, corporate finance, research, issuer-trading staff); information barriers/firewalls; grey and restricted lists; cybersecurity.

elements.title: Supervisory structure: Investment Dealer responsibilities
elements.summary: Obligations of an Investment Dealer when providing products and services, including roles of Executives and the compliance department; analyzing and applying the Supervisor's general responsibilities, including delegation and automation.

outcomes.statement: Requirement to establish a supervision system covering all employees and Approved Persons: written policies and procedures, reasonable assurance of compliance, timely amendment after rule changes, communication and acknowledgment, introductory and continuing training, compliance-notice distribution, annual review of financial/operational P&Ps, record-keeping awareness.

Product due diligence obligations and exemptions: P&Ps, assessment of products made available, product approval, monitoring, exemptions for carrying-broker / execution-clearing-settlement-custody-only accounts.
Executives' responsibility in supervising the dealer: designation requirements, oversight of P&Ps, supervising/directing activities, accountability to Directors, CCO and CFO reporting to the Board.
Executive responsibility for significant areas of risk: financial, operational, corporate finance, trading, wealth management, research.
Implications of internal and external audits on the Investment Dealer.
Common areas of CIRO regulatory/enforcement action against Approved Persons: violating securities/derivatives laws or CIRO requirements, aiding and abetting, failing supervisory obligations.

Role of the compliance function: setting P&Ps; identifying, assessing, advising, acting, communicating, monitoring, escalating and reporting on compliance; CIRO's expectations; escalation responsibility.

Scope of supervision requirements and Supervisor's authority to act for the dealer: reasonable assurance of compliance; assigned supervisory responsibilities (account types, shared premises, sales practices, compliance notices); requirement for authority; client-record amendment controls.

Record-keeping requirements and retention periods relevant to Supervisors: list of Supervisors with qualifications/responsibilities/designation dates, supervisory reports, identified compliance issues, terms of delegation and authority.

Documented controls for delegation: detailed P&Ps, records of delegated tasks, restrictions (delegate qualification, prohibited delegations, delegator retains responsibility), escalation processes, review triggers, examination of delegate reviews, regular reports, at-least-annual review of the delegation process.
Role of automation: how the process works, Supervisor's role and responsibilities, tasks that can and cannot be automated.

Controls for automation processes: manual-review triggers (non-individual applicant, minor, trading authorization, derivatives strategies without knowledge/experience, insider, politically exposed person), periodic testing and auditing.

elements.title: Business and operations
elements.summary: How an Investment Dealer's supervisory structure changes with its business model; analyzing supervisory risks of different account, product and compensation structures.

outcomes.statement: Dealer's responsibility to implement a supervision system adapted to scope and complexity: P&P effectiveness, appointing as many Supervisors as needed, escalation of supervisability concerns, business model factors (products, trading types, due diligence, locations, Supervisor types and functions).
Relationship between a dealer's supervisory system and its internal controls.

Risks, opportunities and requirements for the Investment Dealer associated with business models: advisory (role/responsibilities of Approved Persons, employment arrangements, principal-agent vs employer/employee), portfolio management, managed accounts (PM/APM roles), online advice (operations, obligations, product limits e.g. ETFs), order-execution-only (tools, Direct Electronic Access, CFDs).
Supervisory risks by client type: retail; institutional.
Supervisory risks by account type (retail and institutional): advisory, in-house managed, third-party managed, discretionary, fee-based (non-managed)/wrap, tax-deferred, margin, DEA, DAP/COD/RAP.
Supervisory risks by security type: equities, mutual funds and ETFs, fixed income.
Supervisory risks of complex products: leveraged and inverse ETFs, structured products, asset-backed securities, specialized securities (e.g., cryptocurrencies).
Supervisory risks by derivative type: options, futures contract options, futures, forwards, swaps, contracts for difference, other similar derivatives as described by CIRO.
Supervisory risks of compensation structures: commission-based; fee-based.
Supervisory risks of negotiated flat fees and referral fees.

elements.title: Account approvals
elements.summary: Requirements for account approvals across account types — OEO, discretionary, managed and derivatives accounts — and applying them to specific account-opening situations. The most heavily weighted element.

outcomes.statement: Account approval requirements: collection of client account documentation, prescribed timelines, business-day approval requirement, updates and changes, handling conflicts of interest, non-client account requirements.

Institutional client approval considerations: definition of institutional client, when a qualifying hedger can be classified institutional, the hedger determination, verifying qualification, supervision where not exempt from suitability.
Account appropriateness requirements: putting prospective clients' interests first; appropriate scope and selection of products, services and account relationships.
Client account record requirements: identity-verification documentation, appropriateness-assessment documentation, KYC per CIRO requirements, the account application.

Relationship Disclosure purpose and content: products/services/account types (incl. fee-based, leverage, margin), limitations, fees and compensation, account maintenance conditions, wealth management process, performance benchmarks, impact of fees/turnover/taxes, client relationship model, systematic investment approaches.
Leverage risk disclosure statement: when required, purpose, content.

Margin account agreement purpose and content: repayment and margin-maintenance obligations, interest on debits, dealer rights (pledge assets, use free credits, sell assets/cover shorts, use securities for delivery against short sales, hold collateral), notice requirements, dealer obligation to transact per CIRO/marketplace requirements.

Dealer-related disclosures in the derivatives trading agreement: order-acceptance windows; dealer rights (discretion to accept orders, impose limits or close positions, use free credits and assets as collateral, pledge assets); conditions for applying client property to debts/margin calls; obligations (regulatory reporting of limits/positions, consent before taking the other side, errors and omissions); discretionary authority must be explained and confirmed.

Client-related disclosures in the derivatives trading agreement: comply with CIRO and trading/clearing entity requirements incl. position/exercise limits, maintain margin, pay compensation/interest; acknowledgments — receipt of current derivatives risk disclosure statement, duty to update insider status.

Other requirements of the entity through which a derivative is traded/cleared/issued: exercise-notice deadlines and assignment-notice method; written notice of short-position limits, cash-only terms in last 10 days before expiry, CIRO-imposed rules; client duty to instruct close-out before expiry; minimum margin = greatest of marketplace/clearing house, CIRO, or dealer requirements.

Client cumulative loss limits: applies to accounts trading futures, forwards, CFDs, futures options or similar/highly leveraged products (other than hedging accounts) across advisory, discretionary, managed and OEO; set on a lifetime basis validated annually, or annual basis updated annually.
Letter of Undertaking as an alternative to a trading agreement — institutional clients only: purpose and content.

Supervision of managed accounts: designated Supervisor, specific P&Ps, managed account agreement before opening, written approval per account, record retention, providing the fair-allocation policy to the client.

Managed account agreement purpose and content: client financial situation, knowledge, risk profile, objectives, time horizon; client-imposed restrictions; termination terms — client notice effective on receipt; dealer notice effective not less than 30 days from delivery.

Managed-account conflict-of-interest rules: no trading on managed-account information by dealer/associates/affiliates; no investing in related/connected issuers or dealer-underwritten new issues without prior written client consent/disclosure; no trades with PM/APM/associate accounts or responsible-person-advised funds; no guarantees/loans to responsible persons; fair allocation of investment opportunities.

Managed-account fees and remuneration: no compensating persons based on transaction value/volume; no direct client charges based on volume/value or contingent on profit/performance without written agreement.
A designated Supervisor is prohibited from delegating the performance of discretionary account reviews.

Supervision of discretionary accounts: designated qualified Supervisor(s), specific P&Ps, identification in books and records, agreement before acceptance, designated approval of account and agreement, record retention.

Discretionary account agreement: extent and restrictions of discretionary authority; maximum term 12 months, non-renewable; termination (client — on receipt; dealer — not less than 30 days); conflicts rules incl. prohibition on holding securities of the dealer or affiliates; responsible-person prohibitions.
Account appropriateness assessment in specific situations: advisory, managed, discretionary, leveraged, margin, and derivatives accounts including trading strategies.

OEO account appropriateness: incapacity for online activities (e.g., persistent difficulty with the application), investor seeking advice; restrictions on automated derivatives approvals — CFDs, OTC derivatives, futures strategies, options strategies other than low-risk long calls, long puts, covered calls, protective puts.

elements.title: Account activity
elements.summary: Ongoing risks of different accounts; analyzing situations to identify risks and applying procedures to mitigate or respond.

outcomes.statement: Approaches to supervision: tiered, single centralized, remote-supervision considerations.

Account supervision requirements: identifying clients high-risk to the dealer, clients at high risk of improper market activities, AML/ATF compliance, controls for accessing and amending client records.
Strict supervisory measures for Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions: pre-approval of orders by the Supervisor; daily and monthly review of all client accounts.
Risks of direct order entry without employee intermediation (OEO accounts).
Requirements and conditions for temporary holds to prevent financial exploitation of vulnerable clients.

Retail account supervision situations: daily trading review incl. red flags; monthly review incl. specified account types; detecting concerning practices — unsuitable trading, undue concentration, excessive trading (churning), restricted securities, AP-client conflicts, excessive transfers/cancellations (possible unauthorized trading), high-risk strategies, deterioration of holdings, improper crosses, improper employee trading, front running, account number changes, late payments, outstanding margin calls, undisclosed short sales, manipulative activities, insider trading; required transactional information incl. charges/commissions.
Cross-supervision requirements.
Client mail-hold procedures for retail clients: client authorization, time limits, control and regular review.

Institutional account activity P&Ps: supervision/review of institutional trading; remediation of identified issues; detecting improper activity — manipulative practices, restricted-list trading (securities and derivatives), front running via employee/proprietary accounts, restricted-transfer products, exceeding position/exercise limits, activity around margin thresholds.
Direct market access accounts and supervision for institutional clients, including algorithmic trading.
OEO services exemptions and requirements: trade-review policies, ongoing supervision of automated approvals, direct-entry risks, CFD undertakings.
Derivatives account handling: designating alternate Supervisors where continuous supervision of derivatives trading is required.

Retail derivatives monitoring: excessive intra-day/short-term trading, trading under-margined or beyond limits, exceeding loss limits, exceeding position/exercise limits, speculative trades in hedging accounts, restricted-list/restricted-transfer derivatives, derivatives used to avoid insider trading restrictions, uncovered option exposures, delivery-month exposures.

Derivatives P&Ps: client notifications (approaching expiry, significant derivative changes, business policy changes, new developments); Supervisor approval to solicit derivatives business; no transactions without an executed derivatives trading agreement; handling pending delivery months; detecting insider-avoidance trading; CFD limits — no retail position >0.5% of an issuer's float intra-day/short-term; no CFDs conferring delivery or shareholder rights; directing clients to qualified Approved Persons in business hours.

Supervision of individuals handling managed accounts: conflict-of-interest requirements, fair allocation, compliance assurance, direct supervision of Associate Portfolio Managers (conditions/exceptions), managed account committee requirements.
Importance of preparing and retaining documentation and assessments of clients and client activity.

elements.title: Activities of Approved Persons
elements.summary: Analyzing situations and risks at account opening and during ongoing activity; applying product due diligence, KYC and suitability requirements; client education and disclosure.

outcomes.statement: Supervisor's responsibility for permitted and prohibited activities of Approved Persons: Investment Representatives, Registered Representatives, Associate Portfolio Managers, Portfolio Managers.
Supervisory responsibilities at account opening: OEO, advisory, managed, discretionary, derivatives, margin accounts; use of leverage.
Supervisor's responsibility for suitability determination for retail and institutional clients.
Supervisory responsibilities for ongoing account activity: OEO, leveraged, advisory, managed, discretionary, derivatives accounts.
KYC and product due diligence obligations split between the Investment Dealer and the Approved Person.

Supervisor's role in ensuring client education: purpose of KYC and new-account documentation (financial situation, knowledge, risk profile, needs/objectives, time horizon), KYC-recommendation link, objectives/risk/loss-capacity/performance relationships, full disclosure (debts, goals, cash flows), fluidity of client circumstances, relationship disclosure content incl. fees, institutional application and suitability exemptions, partnership/trust/corporation accounts.
Supervisor must ensure Approved Persons under their supervision have the necessary qualifications for their activities.
Supervisor must ensure information, disclosures and agreements are sent to clients and, where applicable, receipt is acknowledged.

elements.title: Trading and market rules
elements.summary: UMIR obligations; applying regulatory standards to supervision of order placement and gatekeeping responsibilities.

outcomes.statement: Market rules including UMIR: best execution; unacceptable trading; manipulative and deceptive practices; front running; order placement/settlement/delivery; order errors and changes; overdue cash account restrictions; order/trade confirmation requirements incl. fees; order types (basis, bundled, bypass, call market, closing price, dark, jitney, limit, market, market-on-close, multiple client, opening, program trade, special terms, VWAP) [corrected against the official English PDF 2026-06-04]; long/short margin accounts and special margin situations; specialized trading authorizations.
Regulatory standards for the review, acceptance and approval of orders in specific situations.

Trading supervision system obligations: identifying requirements, documentation, training and proficiency, delegation of supervisory/compliance personnel, violation procedures, system reviews, documenting and retaining compliance review results, Board reports.

Regular compliance review of marketplace trading provisions: audit trail, electronic access, specific unacceptable activities, manipulative and deceptive activities, restricted securities, grey-list securities, disclosure, front running, client/principal trading, client priority, best execution, order exposure, time synchronization.

Risk-based supervision system reflecting: Participant size (revenue, market share, exposure, volume), organizational structure, offices, product/service complexity, registrants per location, disciplinary history, risk profile and red flags.
Gatekeeping responsibilities: using the client's typical financial activity patterns to identify suspicious transactions; whistleblower frameworks; reporting obligations to dealers and regulators.

elements.title: Advertisements, sales literature and communications and research reports
elements.summary: Requirements when a dealer or Approved Person communicates with clients, advertises, or produces research; applying these requirements to specific situations.

outcomes.statement: Review and supervision of advertisements, sales literature and correspondence: obligations and best practices; professional titles and trade names; misleading communications; social media restrictions; advisor ranking lists and contests; CASL; off-channel communications; post-use review and sampling; staff familiarity with P&Ps; retention and supervisory record-keeping.

Pre-use/publication approval by a designated Supervisor required for: research reports, market letters, telemarketing scripts, promotional seminar materials, investment fund communications, original advertisements or proofs, any material containing performance reports or summaries used to solicit clients.

Research report disclosure requirements: dissemination P&Ps; conflict disclosures — 1%+ beneficial ownership, analyst/related-person holdings or shorts, prior non-routine payments, investment banking services in preceding 12 months, analyst compensation tied to banking revenues, positions of influence, market making; issuer-paid site visits; rating system and recommendation placement; third-party research; price targets and valuation methods; public appearances; termination of coverage.

Supervision of research production: designated Supervisor review/approval (cross-supervision); controls over pre-publication dissemination; prohibitions — own/holding-company securities, relevant-person relationships with the issuer, investment banking provisions, quiet periods, restricted-list issuers, prohibited inducements; controls over communications to analysts; trading detection/restrictions for relevant persons; designated Executive approval; analyst conduct incl. personal trading and outside activities.

elements.title: Risks associated with Investment Dealer activity and registered locations
elements.summary: Analyzing risk factors relevant to the dealer; applying supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks, including business location supervision.

outcomes.statement: Business location supervision guidelines: scope, planning, business location audit program and related training, risk identification, audit report and follow-up.

Risk factors of different business locations: internal (resources, key-person reliance, principal-agent), reputational (community marketing, outside activities, internal discipline, complaints), business-activity (product/service exposure, lines of business, trade errors), size (risk-weighting, controls, registrant count), client-base risk (clients using the location for inappropriate purposes).

Supervisory requirements for retail distribution risks: complex/opaque/illiquid product sales; inadequate books-and-records control esp. client address changes; failure to meet supervision/control standards incl. advertising review and documented supervision; margin-management failures; weak follow-up on compliance audit findings; products retail investors don't understand; AP failures of obligation and integrity; improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts; commission-based compensation conflicts.
